prospects.thesis: Veeva is the dominant cloud SaaS platform for life sciences CRM, clinical trials, and regulatory workflows, with deep moat via industry-specific data models that are increasingly AI-enabled. The +26.4% 1-month move on bullish options flow and institutional accumulation suggests smart money is positioning ahead of continued Vault expansion and AI-driven upsell into pharma R&D. At $31.8B cap with recurring revenue and a top composite score, it offers the highest-quality exposure to Healthcare AI on the list.

GitLab's DevSecOps platform is monetizing AI aggressively via GitLab Duo, its code-assist and security suite that competes directly with GitHub Copilot in enterprise accounts. The +21.3% run with bullish options and institutional buying reflects growing conviction in seat expansion and Duo attach rates ahead of Q2 print. At 5.4x P/S with a $5.4B cap, valuation is far more digestible than SNOW while the AI narrative is intact.

Edison is a direct beneficiary of Southern California data center power demand and the broader utility bid as VIX rises (+2.06) and Utilities lead the 5D hot-sector tape. Falling 10Y yields (-5bps WoW) support rate-sensitive utility multiples, while bullish institutional flow suggests positioning ahead of rate-case tailwinds and wildfire liability clarity. The +13.2% 1M momentum on a $30.5B defensive name at 1.6x P/S offers attractive risk-adjusted entry.

Meta is monetizing AI at scale — Llama-powered ad targeting is driving ROAS improvements while Reels engagement and WhatsApp Business monetization ramp. The +12.0% 1M gain with bullish options and institutional flow signals confidence into Q2 earnings where capex discipline and ad revenue acceleration should reassert. As mega-cap AI exposure with real cash flows, META anchors the portfolio against the VIX uptick better than smaller AI names.

SAIC provides mission-critical IT modernization, AI/ML integration, and space/cyber services to DoD and civilian agencies — a direct play on the FY26 defense IT budget uplift and JADC2 buildout. At just 0.7x P/S with a $5.0B cap and bullish institutional flow, it's the cheapest way to add Defense Tech diversification, and the +10.2% 1M move suggests contract award momentum. Adds needed theme diversity outside the crowded semiconductor/AI defense trade.

market_pulse.rotation_analysis: Big money is piling into Energy and Consumer Discretionary right now, and the Energy bet especially smells like a policy trade — with the administration continuing to lean into "drill baby drill" permitting, LNG export approvals, and strategic petroleum reserve dynamics, traders are positioning for domestic producers to benefit even if crude prices stay range-bound. The unusual call buying in Consumer Discretionary is more curious given tariff noise, and my read is that smart money is betting tariffs get watered down in negotiations or that the consumer proves more resilient than headlines suggest heading into holiday spending. On the other side, the wall of put buying in Industrials, Consumer Staples, Healthcare, and Utilities is striking — Industrials at a 12x put/call ratio is basically a scream that someone thinks tariff retaliation and slowing global trade will hit manufacturers hard, while Healthcare hedging lines up with ongoing drug pricing pressure, Medicare negotiation expansion, and PBM reform bills quietly moving through Congress. The bearish credit signals in HYG and LQD combined with widening spreads is the tell nobody's talking about: when investment-grade credit starts getting hedged aggressively alongside defensive sectors like Staples and Utilities, it usually means institutions are bracing for either a growth scare or a liquidity event in the next 4-8 weeks, not celebrating a soft landing. The contradiction worth chewing on is Tech — the Nasdaq keeps making highs but XLK puts are stacking up, which tells me the AI trade is getting hedged hard at the top even as retail keeps buying, and unusual volume in Communication Services suggests people are repositioning around antitrust rulings and the Google/Meta regulatory overhang.
